title: Graph Neural Networks Are Not Continuous Across Graph Resolutions
abstract: We show that contrary to conventional wisdom in the community, graph neural networks (GNNs) are not continuous with respect to all natural modes of graph convergence. As a result, GNNs may generate substantially different latent representations for graphs that are very similar. In particular they assign vastly different latent embeddings to graphs that represent the same underlying object at different resolution scales. We trace this failure of continuity back to a structural obstruction arising from commonly used information-propagation schemes. Building on this insight we then derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures which equips models with continuity across scales. The proposed modification enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions and reliable generalization between them. We systematically validate our theoretical findings in a wide range of numerical experiments.
decision_comment:
In this paper, the authors claim that standard GNNs are not continuous with respect to the graph resolution. That is, graphs representing the same underlying object at different coarsening levels can produce drastically different latent embeddings. Following some analysis, the authors suggest a modification to standard propagation based on the Laplace transform to better handle tasks across different scales. All reviewers agree that the paper makes a meaningful contribution to the field. The work is well-motivated, and the proposed approach is clearly presented and supported by a solid theoretical and empirical evaluation.
During the rebuttal phase, the authors thoroughly addressed all concerns raised by the reviewers. Most referees have updated their assessments and voted in favor of acceptance. In light of this consensus and the demonstrated quality of the work, I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: The authors observe that GNNs are not continuous across scales: a GNN can assign very different representations to graphs representing the same object but at different scales. The problem is identified to come from diverging node distances under renormalisation. They propose laplace transform propagation to alleviate this problem.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Overall, great paper. The 

Strengths:
Very interesting and useful observation on GNNs. Nice explaination and elegant fix. The analysis of the GCN renormalized adjacency is very convincing.
sect 6.1 "Each such sub-graph thus effectively behaves as a single node in a coarse grained effective graph G.", good summary.
section 6.1 paragraph "interpolating...": "This is congruent with our intuition: The stronger two nodes are connected, the more they act as a single entity." great observation.
Theorem 7.2 makes the argument very solid.

Weaknesses:
I am still a bit dubious of the demonstration of the discontinuity effect using molecules. Molecular graph are inherently discrete and talking about continuity in this situation does not make perfect sense to me. I would be much more convinced if the phenomenon was described using graphs obtained from different scales discretisation of some continuous manifold. 
The qm7 molecules with hydrogen on/off argument also has something unsatisfying in the fact that the molecules with hydrogen atoms at different distances from their heavy atom has no physical equivalent. If this argument was made with graphs where the varying of the scales did have a physical meaning and all graphs were elements of some space of admissible graphs, the argument would be much stronger.
section 6.1 paragraph heat kernel needs to be reworked, its very hard to follow in the current state. 

In the abstract, please clarify this sentence:
 "We trace this failure of continuity back to a structural obstruction arising from commonly used information-propagation schemes"

There are several uses of the word "coarsified". Isnt "coarsened" better?
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
It seems to me if you want to represent the process of coarsening a molecule to be hydrogen-less, you could also send the hydrogens to infinity. Does the same sort of discontinuities happen then?

Do you have an explaination for discontinuity for GNNs that dont use the same renormalization of the adjacency as GCN has?

reviews.summary: This paper shows that standard message passing in GNNs maps the same graph at different resolution scales to highly different latent embeddings, resulting in loss of generalization performance across less, and provides an alternative information propagation scheme based on laplace transform that encodes different resolution scales to similar latent embeddings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. Claims are supported by both theoretical guarantees and extensive empirical anlaysis.
2. While the paper appears to be technically sound and is generally well-written, I am not sure how well-motivated the problem is, as discussed below. 

Weaknesses: 

1. The concept of 'continuity' is not well defined. While the idea becomes clearer later in the paper with more context, it has been vaguely used at multiple instances in the introduction which makes understanding the main problem (i.e. lack of continuity across graph resolutions) difficult to understand until much later in the paper. 
2. Similarly, the notion of 'similarity' between the original and coarsified graph is unclear. I understand that it is defined here by by the difference in latent embeddings, which do not converge to zero under standard message passing. My question is more in the context of why we should expect them to be similar after message passing. In the example of the Ethane molecule, the nodes and edge (and their weights) between first hop neighbors represent vastly different things in the original (carbon and hydrogen atoms with three C-H bonds between them) and coarsened (carbon+hydrogen 'functional group' with a C-C bond) graphs. These units could have different properties and possibly different inductive biases that need to be modelled. Beyond this, for tasks in other domains, coarsening a graph could change the objects the nodes (or supernodes) represent and the semantics between them that the edges represent. Perhaps a discussion on when having 'continuity' across scales in necessary, and when it may not be a good idea, would elucidate the motivation behind this work better and help identify potential use cases of the proposed framework. 

Note: Misspelling typo in section 7.2 heading (gaurentees -> guarantees).
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Is it  important for nodes to have weights in the context of this paper?
2.  What does the convergence term in Equation 5 signify?
reviews.limitations: None discussed. The scalability of the proposed method seems to be a limitation.
reviews.final_justification: The authors have acknowledged my main concern, i.e. the need to identify when scale continuity is important and promised to add a section to discuss this in detail. Other clarifications provided were also satisfactory.

reviews.summary: This paper identifies a previously underappreciated failure mode of standard GNNs: lack of continuity across graph resolution scales. The root cause of the failure is traced to commonly used normalization schemes (symmetric normalization, renormalized adjacency). To remedy this, the authors propose replacing standard propagation with Laplace-transform propagation matrices. Both proposed theorem and extensive experiments validate the claim.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths
1. The problem identification is clear. The paper cleanly articulates why standard GNNs fail across resolutions. The intuition in Section 5 (effective disconnected limit graph vs. true coarse graph) is compelling and easy to follow. The logical flow from problem identification to diagnosis of the structural obstruction, then proposing solution, finally giving formal guarantees is well-structured.
2. The paper validates its claims across diverse settings: molecular property prediction (QM7), citation networks (Cora, Citeseer), stochastic block models, and continuum limit on torus manifolds. The results effectively demonstrate that Laplace-transform-based models achieve scale continuity while standard GNNs do not.

### Weaknesses
1. The related works are insufficient.  The paper does not discuss diffusion (ODE)-based GNNs that already use heat diffusion for graph. For example, GRAND (Chamberlain et al.), GRAND++ (Thorpe et al.), DiffusionNet (Sharp et al.). And one of the most related work, TIDE (Behmanesh et al.), explicitly used the form $L\exp(-tL)$ as a learnable propagation layer. These methods already implement the core insight (heat kernel) proposed here. The paper's novelty claim of "proposing Laplace-transform propagation" is overstated without acknowledging that the exponential propagation instance already exists in published work. The actual contribution should be more precisely scoped as providing a theoretical framework explaining *why* such diffusion-based propagation has favorable properties across resolutions.
2. The scenario seems highly artificial motivating. The collapsing procedure (Section 4) that moves hydrogen atoms toward heavy atoms is a mathematically constructed interpolation path, not a naturally occurring process. The paper argues in Section 6.1 that this generalizes because any coarsification can be modeled by scaling intra-cluster weights to infinity, but this interpolation path is manually chosen, not intrinsic to the data. 
3. The paper claims that Laplace-transform matrices are  "effectively sparse" due to exponential decay. However, no computational cost analysis like running time, memory usage is provided. For large graph, solving $(L+\lambda I)^{-1}$  is non-trivial. Without scalability evidence, the practical applicability remains unclear.
4. The generalization claim rests on a single small benchmark (QM7). The cross-resolution experiments beyond QM7 (clique expansion, SBM, torus) do verify scale continuity, but they relies entirely on artificial settings with limited practical relevance.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1.  As mentioned in Weakness 1 (W1), Do these ODE-based/TIDE methods also exhibit scale continuity by virtue of using the same propagation matrices? A clear discussion of this relationship would significantly affect my assessment of originality.
2. What is the computational overhead of Laplace-transform propagation (eigendecomposition or resolvent solve) compared to standard message passing on graphs with >10K nodes?
3. The interpolation path is a mathematical construction chosen by scaling intra-cluster weights to infinity. In what real-world scenarios does this specific mode of graph convergence arise naturally, beyond Coulomb-type molecular graphs?
reviews.limitations: The paper does not substantively discuss limitations. No discussion of computational scalability. No discussion of when the proposed convergence mode may not be the relevant notion of graph similarity in practice, i.e., when does the discontinuity formulation will fail.
reviews.final_justification: The problem and the intuition of this work are interesting. During rebuttal, the authors addressed my concerns. So I tend to accept this paper.

reviews.summary: This paper argues that standard graph neural networks (GNNs) are not continuous with respect to graph resolution changes — meaning graphs representing the same underlying object at different coarsening levels can produce drastically different latent embeddings. The authors trace this discontinuity to a structural obstruction in commonly used propagation schemes, and propose replacing standard propagation matrices showing that the resulting architectures are scale-continuous, and validate empirically that these modified GNNs generalise better across resolution scales.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
# Strengths
The paper isolates a precise failure mode rather than a vague "robustness" concern arising from how normalisation interacts with diverging edge weights during coarsening. The QM7 cross-resolution experiments make this immediately tangible, making this paper quite relevant and significant.

The derivations of effective limit propagation schemes for essentially all standard architectures are thorough and the explicit proofs seem to me to be valuable results in themselves.

Finally, it is commendable that the “fix” seems to be architecturally minimal. Replacing the propagation matrix while keeping the rest of the architecture intact (spectral or message-passing) is a clean intervention.


# Weaknesses

For me this looks like an overall very interesting paper with very strong results covered in their tables, but I must say that I have a main weakness to comment on based on the continuity assumption that this paper is based on. Basically, the paper's central claim (that graphs at different resolutions represent "the same underlying object" and therefore should produce similar embeddings) rests in my opinion on a physical prior that is natural in molecular chemistry (atoms aggregating into coarse-grained super-nodes) but I don’t think is actually universally valid. In the QM7 setting, collapsing hydrogen atoms into their nearest heavy atom preserves the physically relevant information because the coarse-graining aligns with the actual hierarchical structure of molecular interactions. But the paper frames this as a general property that GNNs should satisfy across domains, without interrogating when the assumption itself holds. 

For example consider figure 1 and 2. The illustrated graphs are presented as if they should be continuous, but by any traditional graph/network theory perspective, they are fundamentally different, so why do we want to force them to be continuous? I’d like to give an example of a transportation network: merging a densely connected subgraph of intersections into a single node discards routing-level detail that may matter for flow prediction. The paper's notion of scale continuity implicitly assumes that intra-cluster structure is redundant and that the coarse description is a faithful summary of the fine one, but this is a domain-specific and task-specific judgment, not a universal mathematical property. 

The theoretical framework (heat-kernel convergence under edge-weight divergence) looks rigorous, but from what I understand from this paper, it formalises a particular physical intuition about what "same object at different resolutions" should mean, and the paper does not delineate the boundary conditions under which this intuition applies. The experimental validation exclusively uses settings where coarse-graining is semantically lossless (molecules, citation networks with clique expansion, synthetic block models, torus discretisations), which reinforces rather than stress-tests the assumption. 
Because of all of this, I believe a stronger paper would explicitly characterise the class of tasks and graph structures for which scale continuity is desirable, and acknowledge that for tasks where intra-cluster heterogeneity is important, enforcing continuity across resolutions could degrade performance.

Finally, just a minor point on readability, I believe it’s not clear until section 3 what the authors mean by “resolution scales”, which made my initial time reading the paper quite confusing trying to understand what I was reading about.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. As I believe it’s a bit obvious, any counter argument to my previous Weakness would be appreciated.
2. How sensitive are the results to some of the parameter choices made in the paper? In other words, is there a regime where Laplace-transform GNNs lose their advantage?
3. It is my impression that this paper formalises coarsening as collapsing tightly connected clusters into single super-nodes, which seems to be analogous to replacing a clique with a hyperedge that jointly connects all its members. Have the authors considered this connection?
reviews.limitations: The authors mention that “there are many potential societal consequences of our work”, but then proceed to say none of them. In my opinion the fact that this is a more methodologically paper makes it difficult to think about realistic potential negative societal consequences of this work, so I find it weird the construction used by the authors.
reviews.final_justification: I'm happy with the author's rebuttal and what they propose to add in Introduction and Appendix seems reasonable to me. I'll change my score to an Accept. I still think this paper is not a "Strong Accept" because it seems to me that the cases where this continuity property is a desirable one doesn't seem to apply to most of real-world graphs, and the examples they give in appendix are in practice theoretical ones.
